skill.name: web-games
skill.description: Guidance for planning browser games with suitable web game frameworks, rendering APIs, asset loading, performance, audio, and PWA support.
skill.summary: Plan browser game projects with practical framework, performance, WebGPU, asset, audio, and PWA guidance.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: AI review dismissed the external command alerts because SKILL.md:17-30 is a Markdown decision-tree diagram, not executable shell or Ruby. The weak cryptography and system reconnaissance alerts at SKILL.md:3, SKILL.md:28, and SKILL.md:78 are ordinary game development text with no secret access, network calls, or prompt injection evidence.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Markdown Code Fence Flagged as Command Execution
low_findings.description: Static analysis flagged SKILL.md:17 and SKILL.md:30 as Ruby or shell backtick execution. These lines delimit a Markdown code fence that contains a framework decision tree, so there is no executable command behavior.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The suspicious markers are Markdown fence delimiters around prose. The skill only grants Read, Write, Edit, Glob, and Grep tools, with no shell execution instruction.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Weak Cryptography Alert on Description Text
low_findings.description: Static analysis flagged SKILL.md:3 as a weak cryptographic algorithm. The line is metadata describing WebGPU, optimization, framework selection, and PWA topics, with no cryptographic API or secret handling.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The line contains descriptive skill metadata only. I found no evidence of hashing, encryption, signing, or credential processing on that line.

low_findings.title: False Positive: System Reconnaissance Alert on Game Guidance
low_findings.description: Static analysis flagged SKILL.md:28 and SKILL.md:78 as system reconnaissance. These lines discuss hybrid Canvas options and object pooling to avoid garbage collection, not host inspection or data collection.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Both locations are normal browser game design guidance. No commands, filesystem enumeration, environment probing, or network exfiltration behavior appears in context.

content.user_title: Build Browser Games with Web Standards
content.value_statement: Browser game projects need early decisions about frameworks, graphics APIs, assets, audio, and offline support. This skill gives Claude, Codex, and Claude Code a practical checklist for web game planning.
actual_capabilities: Compare Phaser, PixiJS, Three.js, and Babylon.js for 2D and 3D browser games.
Guide WebGPU adoption with WebGL fallback and feature detection.
Recommend asset compression, lazy loading, batching, pooling, and worker strategies.
Outline PWA requirements for offline play, installation, full screen mode, and HTTPS.
Explain browser audio constraints and practical Web Audio API setup.
limitations: It provides planning guidance, not a complete game engine or production asset pipeline.
Framework version notes may need verification against current release documentation.
It does not include source code templates or runnable starter projects.
It does not test performance on target devices or browsers.

use_cases.title: Choose a Browser Game Stack
use_cases.description: Select a suitable framework and rendering approach for a new 2D, 3D, or hybrid web game.

use_cases.title: Plan Web Performance Work
use_cases.description: Create a focused optimization checklist for assets, loading, rendering, memory, and background work.

use_cases.title: Prepare a PWA Game Release
use_cases.description: Review browser requirements for offline play, installation, audio startup, HTTPS, and service worker caching.

prompt_templates.title: Pick a Framework
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner planning a new browser game.
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me choose a browser game framework for a [2D or 3D] game. Compare Phaser, PixiJS, Three.js, and Babylon.js for my needs.

prompt_templates.title: Design a Loading Plan
prompt_templates.prompt: Create an asset loading and compression plan for a browser game with [textures], [audio], and [models]. Include startup, gameplay, and background loading.

prompt_templates.title: Review Performance Risks
prompt_templates.prompt: Audit this browser game plan for performance risks. Focus on draw calls, memory, workers, mobile data, audio, and tab visibility handling.

prompt_templates.title: Plan WebGPU Migration
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced team deciding on graphics architecture.
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a WebGPU adoption plan for my browser game. Include feature detection, WebGL fallback, target browsers, and rollout risks.

output_examples.input: I am building a 2D action game with many sprites and UI effects.
output_examples.output: Recommended direction: use Phaser when you need complete game systems. Use PixiJS when rendering control is the main requirement.

output_examples.input: My browser game loads slowly on mobile networks.
output: Compress textures and audio before release.
Prefetch the next level in the background.

output_examples.input: I want offline play and install support.
output_examples.output: Use a service worker for caching, add a web app manifest, serve through HTTPS, and test full screen behavior on target browsers.

best_practices: Choose the framework based on game type, engine needs, and rendering control.
Use progressive loading and compression before adding more runtime optimization.
Handle browser constraints early, including audio unlock, tab visibility, and HTTPS.
anti_patterns: Selecting WebGPU without a fallback plan for unsupported browsers.
Loading all game assets before the first playable screen.
Ignoring mobile bandwidth, autoplay limits, and background tab throttling.

faq.question: Does this skill generate complete games?
faq.answer: No. It guides planning, framework selection, optimization, browser constraints, and release preparation.

faq.question: Which frameworks does it cover?
faq.answer: It covers Phaser, PixiJS, Three.js, Babylon.js, Raw Canvas, WebGL, and WebGPU decision points.

faq.question: Can it help with mobile browser performance?
faq.answer: Yes. It highlights asset compression, lazy loading, data limits, pooling, batching, and worker usage.

faq.question: Does it include PWA guidance?
faq.answer: Yes. It covers service workers, web app manifests, HTTPS, offline play, installation, and full screen mode.

faq.question: Does it require shell commands?
faq.answer: No. The skill guidance is Markdown content and does not request command execution.

faq.question: Should browser support claims be verified?
faq.answer: Yes. Version and market share information should be checked against current browser compatibility data.
